prompt: Find the work in the corpus that gives a month-by-month embryology with this exact sequence of transformations: in the first month a clot of blood the color of a crushed piece of spleen; in the third month the spleen-like mass becomes a single red egg of blood; in the fourth month the egg becomes a Śivaliṅga, hollow in the middle, inhabited by Oṃkāra and the subtle form; in the fifth month the Śivaliṅga becomes an illusory form; in the sixth month the illusory form becomes fire; in the seventh month the fire becomes 'like an ivory doll'; in the eighth month breath emerges from Oṃkāra together with flesh, bone, nails, and hair; and at ten months the yoga is complete and the child emerges from the womb. Quote the embryological sequence verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This particular tantric embryology — the third-month single egg of blood, the fourth-month transformation of the egg into a hollow Śivaliṅga inhabited by Oṃkāra, the seventh-month 'ivory doll' simile, and the closing 'completion of yoga' at ten months — is unique to this Old Javanese Śaiva work and is not reproduced in the sibling tattvas (Vrhaspatitattva, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna), in the Sanskrit smṛtis, or in the Old Javanese Buddhist works (Kalpabuddha, Kuñjarakarṇa, San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan). The Śivaliṅga-shaped fourth-month embryo and the ivory-doll seventh-month simile are the dead giveaways.

prompt: Find the work in the corpus that teaches a five-syllable mantra called the Pañcakāṇḍa in which each syllable is assigned to one of the five gross elements with this exact mapping: in the syllable jaḥ, earth is to be known; in the syllable taḥ, the waters are established; in the syllable kiḥ is the great fire; in the syllable uṃ one should place the wind; and joined with the syllable phaṭ for the ether — recited for the destruction of great sins, granting the world of Śiva to the wise reciter. Quote the two ślokas that give this element-to-syllable mapping verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The Pañcakāṇḍa with this exact five-syllable element assignment (jaḥ-earth, taḥ-water, kiḥ-fire, uṃ-wind, phaṭ-ether) is a signature mantric system of this Old Javanese Śaiva text. The sibling tattvas (Vrhaspatitattva, Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna) and the Sanskrit smṛtis do not contain this five-syllable bhūta-mantra schema. The combination of the unusual seed-syllables jaḥ/taḥ/kiḥ together with the destruction-of-great-sins fruit and the Śiva-loka attainment makes the passage uniquely identifiable.

prompt: Find the work in the corpus that closes with a purification-and-exorcism rite addressed to a son of Śiva who is depicted in a magical drawing with a discus in his left hand and a mace in his right, with these exact ritual materials: ivory bamboo (ampel gading) bearing the rajah of the deity, a white-and-yellow ajuman set, a sucī offering with a white crested chicken as its main dish, water held in a copper vessel (sanku), sudhāmala flowers with pras and santun offerings, a monetary offering of eleven hundred, and samsam of katima leaves; and that lists the rite's protections as effective against an epidemic, epilepsy, spirit affliction, and destruction caused by animals. Quote the deity's invocation that hails him as 'Destroyer of all poisons, Lord of Kāladṛṅga and Dṛṅgī, who makes opposing diseases faint, who gives life to the three worlds,' and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This closing exorcism rite — with the very specific ritual prescription (ivory bamboo, white crested chicken, copper sanku, monetary offering of 1100, katima leaves), the iconographic discus-and-mace placement, the named ailments (epidemic, epilepsy, spirit affliction, animal destruction), and the rare epithet 'Lord of Kāladṛṅga and Dṛṅgī' — is unique to this Old Javanese Śaiva text. None of the sibling tattvas, the Sanskrit smṛtis, the Old Javanese Buddhist works, or Slokāntara contains a paṅlukatan rite for this specific son of Śiva with these exact materials and protections, nor the Kāladṛṅga epithet.
